05 Step 3 Backtest the Previous Month or Quarter - YouTube.mp4
Once you've figured out the metrics that you're gonna be tracking on a weekly basis, the next step is to simply go back and fill in a dashboard for at least the previous month, or better yet, the previous quarter. This process of measuring in reverse is known as backtesting. But why would you want to do that, right? Why would I have you track data for a time period that has already past? Well, there are two big reasons. Reason number one, this is a good way to find out if you can even get the data for the metrics that you have selected. Now, I can't tell you how many times my team and I have come up with great ideas for KPIs we believe that we should be tracking.
Only to discover that we either can't get the data we need, or getting the data is simply too manual or too time-consuming to be worth the effort. If that happens, don't worry. Drop the metric for now and look to add it back in when the data source is more readily available. The second reason you want to back test your metrics for at least a month and again ideally a quarter is so that you can use your actual data to benchmark your targets. For example, let's say your marketing team is tracking new qualified leads. Well, how do you know what... good is, right? Is 10 leads good? 100 leads? Again, how do you know? You can guess, or you can look back over the previous few months to see what it... has been, and simply ask, what months were good, and what months did we fall short? Obviously, this method isn't perfect, but at least you're leveraging actual data rather than just gut. And the best way to get that data is to measure your actuals by looking back. Now that you have at least four weeks of back-tested data, you're free to move on to the next step.
